title: Shippo tokens should not be disclosed
description: Shippo is a multi-carrier shipping API platform that provides unified access to USPS, FedEx, UPS, DHL, Canada Post, and 80+ global carriers for shipping label generation, real-time rate calculation, package tracking, address validation, customs documentation, and carrier account management. Shippo API tokens authenticate programmatic access to shipping operations and come in two types: shippo_live_ tokens for production shipping with real carrier charges, and shippo_test_ tokens for sandbox development. These tokens grant extensive permissions including: creating shipping labels with carrier-specific rate shopping across multiple services (First Class, Priority, Express, International), purchasing postage and generating tracking numbers, accessing real-time package tracking data and delivery status, validating and standardizing shipping addresses with USPS CASS certification, managing customs forms (CN22, CN23, EEL, PFC) for international shipments, configuring carrier accounts and negotiated rate integration, creating batch shipments for high-volume fulfillment, accessing transaction history and shipping analytics, managing return labels and reverse logistics, and configuring webhook notifications for shipment events. Exposing Shippo API tokens enables attackers to: create unauthorized shipping labels incurring direct carrier costs charged to your account ($3-$100+ per label depending on service and destination), exhaust shipping credit balances or exceed spending limits causing fulfillment disruption, access customer shipping addresses and personally identifiable information (PII) violating privacy regulations, steal delivery tracking data revealing business shipping volumes and customer locations, generate fraudulent return labels for unauthorized product returns, modify or cancel existing shipments disrupting order fulfillment, access carrier account credentials and negotiated shipping rates (competitive intelligence), create international customs declarations with incorrect valuations affecting duties and tariffs, abuse carrier accounts leading to account suspension or rate increases, intercept webhook notifications containing order fulfillment data, scrape shipping analytics revealing business metrics (daily volume, geographic distribution, average package weights), create labels to unauthorized addresses for package interception or fraud, exhaust API rate limits (up to 100 requests/second) blocking legitimate operations, and incur financial liability for shipping costs that can accumulate rapidly for international or overnight services.
recommendation: Never hardcode Shippo API tokens in source code, configuration files, or version control. Immediately deactivate the exposed API token from Shippo dashboard (Settings → API → Revoke Token), generate a new replacement token, and audit recent API activity for unauthorized label creation or suspicious access patterns. Store API tokens securely using environment variables (SHIPPO_API_TOKEN, SHIPPO_LIVE_TOKEN, SHIPPO_TEST_TOKEN), secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager), or encrypted configuration. Implement least privilege by using separate API tokens for different applications, environments (production vs test), or permission scopes. Use test tokens (shippo_test_) exclusively for development and staging to avoid production charges. Rotate API tokens regularly (every 90 days recommended) and monitor shipping activity through Shippo's transaction dashboard for unusual patterns. Enable webhook signature validation to prevent unauthorized webhook abuse. Review carrier account connections and ensure no unauthorized accounts were added. Check shipping address history for unexpected destinations. Monitor shipping costs and set up billing alerts for unusual spending patterns. Implement rate limiting and shipping volume caps in applications to prevent abuse even if tokens are compromised. Use IP whitelisting if your infrastructure has static addresses. Maintain separate tokens for different fulfillment centers, warehouses, or business units to isolate potential compromise. Store carrier account credentials securely and never expose them through API responses or logs.
impact: Critical security risk - Exposed Shippo API tokens grant unauthorized access to multi-carrier shipping infrastructure enabling financial loss through unauthorized label purchases ($3-$100+ per label, potentially thousands in minutes), shipping credit exhaustion disrupting order fulfillment during peak periods, customer privacy violations from accessing shipping addresses and tracking data (GDPR, CCPA, PCI-DSS violations), theft of competitive shipping intelligence (volume metrics, carrier rates, geographic distribution), fraudulent return label generation, shipment interception through address manipulation, carrier account suspension from abuse, compliance violations for customs documentation fraud, operational disruption from API rate limit exhaustion, webhook hijacking revealing order fulfillment workflows, and financial liability for shipping costs. For e-commerce businesses, compromised tokens can halt all order fulfillment, expose customer addresses, and incur significant carrier charges within hours.

compliant.description: Using environment variables for Shippo credentials
compliant.code:
import os
import shippo

# Load Shippo token from environment
SHIPPO_API_TOKEN = os.environ.get('SHIPPO_API_TOKEN')

shippo.config.api_key = SHIPPO_API_TOKEN

checks.message: Shippo live API token detected in source code. This token provides production access to Shippo's multi-carrier shipping platform with real carrier charges. Exposing this token allows attackers to: create unauthorized shipping labels incurring direct costs ($3-$100+ per label) charged to your account and rapidly depleting shipping credits (potential losses of thousands within minutes for high-volume abuse), access customer shipping addresses and personally identifiable information (PII) including names, addresses, phone numbers, and email addresses violating GDPR (fines up to €20M or 4%), CCPA (penalties up to $7,500 per violation), and PCI-DSS requirements for e-commerce, steal delivery tracking data revealing business shipping volumes, geographic customer distribution, and fulfillment patterns (competitive intelligence theft), generate fraudulent return labels enabling unauthorized product returns and reverse logistics abuse, modify or cancel legitimate shipments disrupting order fulfillment and causing customer service issues during peak seasons, access connected carrier accounts (USPS, FedEx, UPS, DHL) and negotiated shipping rates (proprietary pricing intelligence), create international customs forms with incorrect valuations affecting import duties, tariffs, and regulatory compliance, abuse carrier accounts leading to rate increases, account suspension, or termination of shipping contracts, intercept webhook notifications containing order fulfillment data, shipment status updates, and delivery confirmation, scrape shipping analytics and transaction history revealing daily volumes (e.g., 1000 labels/day indicates business scale), average package weights, destination patterns, and service preferences, create labels to attacker-controlled addresses for package interception, mail fraud, or tracking number harvesting, exhaust API rate limits (100 requests/second) blocking legitimate shipping operations during critical fulfillment windows, and incur significant financial liability since shipping costs accumulate rapidly especially for international shipments ($50-$200/label), overnight services ($20-$80/label), or large packages. IMMEDIATELY deactivate this token from Shippo dashboard at https://apps.goshippo.com/settings/api (Settings → API → [Token Name] → Revoke), generate a new replacement token, audit recent API activity in Shippo transaction dashboard for unauthorized shipments checking for: unusual shipping volumes (e.g., 100+ labels in short period), unexpected destination addresses or countries, high-value services (overnight, international), unfamiliar carrier accounts, suspicious address validation requests, webhook configuration changes, and API calls from unexpected IP addresses or geographic locations. Review shipping costs and billing for unauthorized charges. Check carrier account connections for unauthorized additions. Verify webhook endpoints haven't been modified to exfiltrate data. Store API tokens securely using environment variables (SHIPPO_API_TOKEN, SHIPPO_LIVE_TOKEN) never hardcoded in source code, secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager stores at aws secretsmanager get-secret-value --secret-id shippo/live-token, HashiCorp Vault at vault kv get secret/shippo/token, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager), or encrypted configuration. Implement least privilege by using separate tokens for: different applications, environments (use shippo_test_ tokens exclusively for development/staging), fulfillment centers or warehouses, business units or brands, and permission scopes. Use test tokens (shippo_test_) in all non-production environments to prevent accidental production charges. Rotate tokens every 90 days. Enable webhook signature validation using webhook secrets (whsec_) to prevent unauthorized webhook abuse. Monitor shipping patterns and set alerts for: volume spikes (>50% baseline), cost anomalies (spending >$500/day), unusual destinations (new countries), off-hours activity, unexpected carriers. Implement application-level controls: rate limiting (max 50 labels/minute), shipping volume caps (max 1000/day), destination whitelisting (allowed countries), service restrictions (block overnight unless needed), address validation enforcement, and cost thresholds requiring approval (>$100/label). Use IP whitelisting in Shippo settings if infrastructure has static IPs. Maintain audit logs of token usage. Review and revoke old/unused tokens quarterly to minimize attack surface.

Shippo test API token detected in source code. While this is a test/sandbox token that doesn't incur real carrier charges, exposing it still presents security risks. Test tokens provide access to: Shippo's sandbox environment for development and testing, address validation testing with real USPS CASS-certified data, rate calculation testing with actual carrier pricing structures, label generation testing creating test tracking numbers, webhook testing and endpoint validation, customs form generation and validation, carrier account integration testing, and API functionality exploration. Exposing test tokens enables attackers to: abuse sandbox resources and exceed test environment quotas, access test shipment data and address information used for development, analyze API functionality and identify potential vulnerabilities for production attacks, test attack vectors and exploitation techniques in sandbox before targeting production, exhaust test API rate limits disrupting development and QA activities, scrape carrier rate structures and pricing algorithms from test responses, access test webhook configurations revealing application architecture, identify connected carrier accounts and integration patterns, and use test token patterns to craft attacks against production tokens (pattern analysis). Additionally, developers often: use similar token formats/storage between test and production (if test token is hardcoded, production likely is too), store test and production tokens in the same configuration files or secret managers, use test tokens in commits that also accidentally include production credentials, maintain test tokens that eventually get promoted to production environments, and follow similar security practices across environments making test token exposure a canary for production vulnerabilities. IMMEDIATELY rotate this test token from Shippo dashboard at https://apps.goshippo.com/settings/api (Settings → API → Test Token → Regenerate), audit source code and version control history for any accidentally committed production tokens (search for 'shippo_live_'), review test environment configurations to ensure production credentials aren't present, verify test and production environments are properly segregated, and implement secure token management practices. Store test tokens using environment variables (SHIPPO_TEST_TOKEN) never hardcoded, use separate configuration files for test vs production (test.env, production.env) that are never committed together, implement token validation in CI/CD pipelines to detect accidental production token commits, use secret scanning tools (git-secrets, truffleHog, GitHub secret scanning), maintain clear separation between test and production infrastructure, rotate test tokens regularly (every 90 days) even though they're for sandbox use, use descriptive token names in Shippo dashboard to identify environment usage (e.g., 'Development Token', 'CI/CD Test Token'), implement least privilege even for test tokens, and monitor test API usage for abuse patterns. Remember: test token exposure often indicates broader security issues and can be leveraged to attack production systems.
